skill.name: async-python-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for designing, reviewing, and improving Python asyncio code with clear concurrency patterns, cancellation handling, timeouts, queues, and async testing practices.
skill.summary: Apply practical asyncio patterns for non-blocking Python services and I/O-heavy workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The skill is documentation-only and no malicious intent, prompt injection text, exfiltration logic, or self-executing payload was found. Most static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline backticks, and descriptive text. A medium documentation risk remains because the async database example builds SQL with f-strings that users could copy into production.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe SQL String Interpolation in Example Code
medium_findings.description: The async database example constructs SQL queries with f-strings. The shown parameter is typed as an integer, but this pattern can teach unsafe query construction if copied with untrusted input. Use parameterized queries in production examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The f-string SQL construction is directly visible in the example. The risk is documentation-level rather than active exploitation because the skill contains examples, not executable runtime code.

low_findings.title: Network Examples Use Public URLs
low_findings.description: The playbook includes example API and documentation URLs for async HTTP patterns. These are not automatic network calls by the skill, but users should replace sample endpoints and follow target site policies before running generated examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly present in documentation examples. They are legitimate teaching examples, so the operational risk is low unless users run adapted code without review.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backticks as Ruby or shell execution. Review showed Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and Python examples, with no shell command invocation by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations correspond to Markdown syntax and Python code blocks. No evidence found of actual shell execution, subprocess use, or command injection behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Alerts Are Benign Example Text Except SQL Pattern
low_findings.description: Several blocker alerts point to ordinary async example code such as task creation, errors, client IDs, and best-practice text. The meaningful issue in this group is reported separately as unsafe SQL string interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are readable documentation and sample Python code with no system reconnaissance behavior. Confidence is high after line-level review, while the SQL-related lines are handled as a separate medium finding.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert points to the skill description, which discusses Python asyncio and high-performance applications. No cryptographic algorithm or crypto API usage appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain descriptive metadata with no cryptographic code or algorithm names. No evidence found for weak cryptography in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable SQL Query Construction Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates query construction with embedded variables. This is not malicious, but it is a dangerous pattern when adapted to real database drivers and untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pattern is visible in the async database example. The exploitability depends on user adaptation, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for real-world impact.

content.value_statement: Async Python code often fails through blocked event loops, unmanaged tasks, and unclear cancellation behavior. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for asyncio services, I/O workflows, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Explains core asyncio concepts such as event loops, coroutines, tasks, futures, async context managers, and async iterators.
Provides patterns for gather, task creation, queues, semaphores, locks, timeouts, and cancellation handling.
Guides async HTTP, web scraping, WebSocket, database, and background processing designs.
Highlights common asyncio mistakes such as missing await calls and blocking the event loop.
Includes testing guidance for async functions with pytest-asyncio and timeout assertions.
Helps choose concurrency patterns based on I/O workload, runtime constraints, and error-handling needs.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run benchmarks or inspect live event-loop behavior by itself.
Some examples use placeholder endpoints and simulated clients that need production hardening.
It is not a full security guide for databases, authentication, scraping compliance, or API rate limits.
CPU-bound workloads still need multiprocessing, native extensions, or worker pools beyond basic asyncio patterns.

use_cases.title: Design an Async API Service
use_cases.description: Plan request handlers, background tasks, timeouts, and connection pooling for a FastAPI or aiohttp service.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Async Code
use_cases.description: Find blocked event loops, unmanaged tasks, missing awaits, weak cancellation handling, and unsafe concurrency limits.

use_cases.title: Create Async Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Add pytest-asyncio tests for timeouts, exceptions, concurrent operations, and cancellation cleanup behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Explain My Async Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to explain how my coroutine flow should work. Identify where I need await, tasks, and a single asyncio.run entry point.

prompt_templates.title: Convert I/O Code to Async
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to convert this I/O-bound Python workflow to asyncio. Include timeouts, error handling, and a limit on concurrent work.

prompt_templates.title: Review Concurrency Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to review this async code for blocked event loops, task leaks, cancellation bugs, missing backpressure, and unsafe shared state.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Async Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the async-python-patterns skill to design an async Python service for this workload. Compare queues, gather, semaphores, connection pools, retries, and graceful shutdown.

output_examples.input: Review an async HTTP worker that sends many requests at once.
output: Concurrency should be capped with a semaphore or connector limits.
Each request should have a timeout and clear exception handling.
The session should be reused through an async context manager.
Use bounded queues to provide backpressure.
Start a fixed worker set and handle cancellation during shutdown.
Call task_done after each item and wait on queue.join before closing.

output_examples.input: Add tests for an async function with a slow dependency.
output: Assert timeout behavior with the expected timeout exception.
Test both successful completion and cleanup after cancellation.

best_practices: Clarify whether the workload is I/O-bound before choosing asyncio.
Add timeouts, cancellation handling, and concurrency limits before production use.
Use async context managers for clients, sessions, pools, and cleanup-sensitive resources.
anti_patterns: Do not call blocking functions directly inside async functions.
Do not create unbounded task lists for external services or databases.
Do not copy database examples without replacing string-built SQL with parameterized queries.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Python code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for asyncio patterns. Users decide what code to run.

faq.question: Can it help with FastAPI or aiohttp projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers async APIs, HTTP clients, connection pooling, timeouts, and service design choices.

faq.question: Is asyncio useful for CPU-bound work?
faq.answer: Usually no. CPU-bound work often needs multiprocessing, native code, or executor pools.

faq.question: Does it cover cancellation and shutdown?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes cancellation handling, task cleanup, queues, and graceful async control flow.

faq.question: Are the sample network URLs production ready?
faq.answer: No. They are examples. Replace them with approved endpoints and follow rate limits and site policies.

faq.question: What should users review before copying database examples?
faq.answer: Users should replace string-built SQL with parameterized queries and follow their database driver guidance.
